title: Wet-felted beret
subtitle: A shaped merino beret felted flat and blocked over a plate to a 24 cm crown
excerpt: Felt a flat disc of merino roving, cut a hole for the head opening, and block over an upturned dinner plate to produce a beret with a shallow crown and a firm turned brim. One afternoon to felt and block, overnight to dry.
sourceNotes: Disc-method beret construction is a standard workshop technique used across European felting guilds. The plate-blocking method derives from traditional Basque beret production, where felt circles were blocked over wooden forms. Observation-led adaptation for kitchen-table felting.

glossaryTerms.definition: Combed and drawn wool fibres aligned in a continuous rope, ready for spinning or felting. In wet felting, wisps are pulled from the roving strip and laid in cross-hatched layers before wetting and agitation.

The stage of wet felting in which the fibres have already locked together and the felt is worked aggressively to shrink and compact it to the final size and density. Fulling follows initial felting and is where the shape consolidates.

content.text: A wet-felted beret is made from a flat disc of

that is felted, cut, and then blocked over a plate to produce the characteristic shallow dome and turned brim. The disc method gives consistent head-opening control: you choose the size of the hole you cut, then felt the brim down into a firm band. Merino is the right fibre for a first beret because it felts predictably and produces a dense, smooth surface. Expect 40 to 50 percent shrinkage in diameter from the raw layout to the finished hat.

items.name: Hot water and cold water for shock rinse
Olive-oil soap or pH-neutral wool wash, liquid or grated
Bamboo rolling mat or bubble wrap sheet
Cotton tea towel for pressing layer
Dinner plate, 24 cm diameter, for blocking

content.text: Mark a circle 55 cm in diameter on your mat. Pull wisps of roving approximately 2 cm wide and lay them across the circle in one direction, overlapping the edges by 3 cm. Build a second layer at 90 degrees to the first. Add a third layer in the original direction. Three layers of merino roving laid this way gives a beret with good density; four layers produces a slightly stiffer brim. Keep the layers even across the whole disc, including the centre and the edges.

Mix hot water with a small amount of soap in a jug. Pour over the laid roving in a circular pattern, working from the centre outward. Place the tea towel over the wet roving and press the water through with your palms using flat, even pressure. Press from the centre toward the edges, then across the full disc. The roving should lie flat and wet through with no dry patches. Gently fold the overhanging edges onto the top surface and press them down.

Rub the surface through the tea towel with your palms in circular motions for 5 minutes. Lift the cloth, check the surface, and rub directly on the wet roving for another 5 minutes. The fibres should begin to knit together and the surface should look matted rather than fluffy. Test by pinching the surface: if fibres lift away freely, continue rubbing. If the surface resists pinching and a few fibres lift but the layer holds, you are ready to roll.

Roll the disc up in the bamboo mat and roll the bundle back and forth on a firm surface for 5 minutes. Unroll, turn the disc 90 degrees, and roll again. Repeat until the disc has shrunk to approximately 35 cm in diameter and the surface is dense and firm. This is the

stage. Shock the felt by plunging it into cold water for 30 seconds, then back into hot water. The thermal shock tightens the fibre lock. Continue rolling until the disc is 30 to 32 cm in diameter and feels firm when squeezed.

Find the centre of the disc by folding it in quarters and marking the midpoint. Cut a circle at the centre: start with a 12 cm diameter opening for a head circumference of 54 to 56 cm (measure the intended wearer and adjust; the opening will stretch slightly during blocking). Cut slowly and check the fit before cutting further. Felt the cut edge by rubbing it between wet palms to seal the raw fibres.

Place the damp beret over the upturned dinner plate with the crown sitting on the plate base. Smooth the brim down evenly all around and roll the edge under to form a band 2 to 3 cm wide. Pin the brim in place if it springs up. Leave to dry overnight on the plate. Once dry the beret holds its shape without the plate. Hand-wash cool with wool wash and re-block on the plate while damp if needed.

items.problem: The disc shrank unevenly and is oval rather than round
items.cause: Rolling happened in only one direction, compressing the fibres in a single axis
items.fix: Turn the disc 90 degrees between every roll sequence. Roll in at least four directions across the process to maintain even shrinkage.

items.problem: The head opening is too large and the beret sits low on the head
items.cause: Too much was cut from the centre disc
items.fix: Wet the brim thoroughly and rub the edge directly for 5 to 10 minutes, which will tighten the opening by 1 to 2 cm. A small amount of additional shrinkage is possible at this stage.

items.problem: The felt is thin or uneven in patches
items.cause: The roving wisps were laid unevenly
items.fix: Wet a small wisp of merino roving and place it over the thin area. Needle-felt it in with a coarse needle to bond it to the base layer. Rewet the surface and rub it smooth.
